Advisor Meeting • April 2026

RF Project

Open Questions for Discussion

Adaptive inference for sparse field reconstruction under coherence time constraints — clarifying the datasets, the scenario, and the routing feasibility.

Mehmet Dulkadiroglu • UIC ECE


Scroll
01

RadioMapSeer Dataset

The primary dataset for this project. Each sample maps a city layout and transmitter position to a pathloss radio map. The reconstruction task: recover the full map from sparse signal measurements.

RECONSTRUCTION TASK RadioMapSeer • Urban pathloss
Input City map + Tx + sparse measurements
Output Full 256×256 pathloss map
Samples 56,080 maps
Resolution 1 meter / pixel
Cities 701 real layouts
Propagation Ray-tracing (DPM/IRT)
Pixel values Signal strength (dB)
Temporal None (static)
Key property: Each radio map is a static snapshot — computed once for a fixed environment via ray-tracing. There is no channel variation over time, no user mobility, and no Doppler effect. The reconstruction task has no natural deadline.
02

HRRR Wind Dataset

A secondary dataset used in Bilginer’s REPA paper. The same sparse reconstruction task, but on wind fields instead of radio maps.

RECONSTRUCTION TASK Oral & Koyuncu, 2025 (REPA)
Data source NOAA HRRR v4
Sample 2 × 128 × 128 (U, V wind)
Sensors 4–16 of 256 patches
Temporal None (static)
Key property: Also static hourly snapshots. No temporal sequence, no mobility, no Doppler. Same structural limitation as RadioMapSeer for the coherence time motivation.
03

Open Concerns

Three things I’d like to clarify before proceeding. Each concern shows the full email thread with the relevant parts highlighted.

1

What exactly will the ML models reconstruct — and where does coherence time apply?

From: Koyuncu, Erdem
RF projesi Apr 1
Mehmet Merhaba,

Sabah bahsettigimiz gibi RF problemi uzerinde calisir misin? Bilginer su sekilde modeller train etti: Modele 4 sample giriyor, butun field reconstruct ediliyor. Senin router modeliyle bu modellerde early exit yapacagiz…. Sorun olursa lutfen sor.

Aslinda bu konuda baska modeller de var onlari da egitebiliriz…

Bilginer, dataset vs yi Mehmet’e tanitir misin?
From: Dulkadiroglu, Mehmet
Re: RF projesi Apr 5
Hocam merhaba,

Routing setup’imizdaki motivasyonla ilgili bir soru sormak istiyorum.

Bilginer’in makalesinde Tablo 1’de bahsettigi inference sureleri halihazirda oldukca kisa gorunuyor. Mesela REPA 32’lik bir batchte yaklasik 59 milisaniye suruyor (sample basina ~1.9 ms). Diger bir yontem olan ET’de sample basina ~3 milisaniye suruyor.

Router ekligimiz durumda onun da ayri latency’si ve complexity’si olacak. Bu tradeoff’un hangi senaryoda anlamli bir avantaj saglayabilecegini netlestirmek istedim. Bu konuda aklinizda real-time, streaming veya repeated inference use case’i var mi?
From: Koyuncu, Erdem
Re: RF projesi Apr 6
Real-time diyelim… Mesela Antenler olctukce real time butun field’i reconstruct ediyoruz. Bahsettigin sureler aslinda uzun sureler kanalin degisim hizini dusundun zaman. <Channel coherence time< diye yazip kisaca okuyabilirsiniz. Kanal coherence’i kaybettikten sonra zaten field’i reconstruct etmenin bir anlami yok.
From: Koyuncu, Erdem
Re: RF projesi (follow-up) Apr 6
Pratik olarak kanallarin coherence time’i 1/doppler spread. Yani sen ne kadar hareketliysen kanal o kadar az coherent. Mesela 100 km/saat hizla bir arabanin icindeysen senin icin kanal 3 milisaniye coherent ancak, dolayisiyla kanali yeniden olcmen lazim.

Bir yerde oturuyorsan tabii ki doppler yok, ama etraftaki tipler hareket ettigi icin kanal yine de degisiyor, yani tas catlasin birkac yuz milisaniye coherence time alirsin. Yani cok cok buyuk bir marjin yok.

Inference time 2 saniyeyse mesela ve coherence time 200ms ise Sen inference yapana kadar kanal degisiyor… Real time bir sey yapmak istiyorsan neural networkun cop…. Tabii ne yapmak istedigine bagli. Ornek vermek istedim.
The first email describes the task using Bilginer’s models and the HRRR wind dataset. The follow-up emails introduce channel coherence time and Doppler spread — wireless channel concepts.

But the HRRR wind dataset has nothing to do with wireless channels, Doppler, or coherence time — it’s static hourly wind snapshots. RadioMapSeer is closer to RF, but also static — no channel variation over time.

In the emails, “kanal” refers to a wireless communication channel that changes over time due to mobility. But which channel are we reconstructing? A channel that decorrelates in 3–200ms would require time-varying channel measurements (e.g., CSI matrices sampled as a user moves) — neither dataset provides this.

What I’d like to clarify: How do static field reconstruction and time-varying channel coherence connect? Which “channel” is being reconstructed, and what does the input/output look like in the coherence time scenario?
2

What is the concrete scenario, and which dataset supports it?

From: Koyuncu, Erdem
RF projesi Apr 1
Mehmet Merhaba,

Sabah bahsettigimiz gibi RF problemi uzerinde calisir misin? Bilginer su sekilde modeller train etti: Modele 4 sample giriyor, butun field reconstruct ediliyor. Senin router modeliyle bu modellerde early exit yapacagiz…. Sorun olursa lutfen sor.

Aslinda bu konuda baska modeller de var onlari da egitebiliriz…

Bilginer, dataset vs yi Mehmet’e tanitir misin?

Bilginer’in makalesinde Tablo 1’de bahsettigi inference sureleri halihazirda oldukca kisa gorunuyor. Mesela REPA 32’lik bir batchte yaklasik 59 milisaniye suruyor (sample basina ~1.9 ms). Diger bir yontem olan ET’de sample basina ~3 milisaniye suruyor.

Router ekligimiz durumda onun da ayri latency’si ve complexity’si olacak. Bu tradeoff’un hangi senaryoda anlamli bir avantaj saglayabilecegini netlestirmek istedim. Bu konuda aklinizda real-time, streaming veya repeated inference use case’i var mi?

Re: RF projesi Apr 6
Real-time diyelim… Mesela Antenler olctukce real time butun field’i reconstruct ediyoruz. Bahsettigin sureler aslinda uzun sureler kanalin degisim hizini dusundun zaman. <Channel coherence time< diye yazip kisaca okuyabilirsiniz. Kanal coherence’i kaybettikten sonra zaten field’i reconstruct etmenin bir anlami yok.
From: Koyuncu, Erdem
Re: RF projesi (follow-up) Apr 6
Pratik olarak kanallarin coherence time’i 1/doppler spread. Yani sen ne kadar hareketliysen kanal o kadar az coherent. Mesela 100 km/saat hizla bir arabanin icindeysen senin icin kanal 3 milisaniye coherent ancak, dolayisiyla kanali yeniden olcmen lazim.

Bir yerde oturuyorsan tabii ki doppler yok, ama etraftaki tipler hareket ettigi icin kanal yine de degisiyor, yani tas catlasin birkac yuz milisaniye coherence time alirsin. Yani cok cok buyuk bir marjin yok.

Inference time 2 saniyeyse mesela ve coherence time 200ms ise Sen inference yapana kadar kanal degisiyor… Real time bir sey yapmak istiyorsan neural networkun cop…. Tabii ne yapmak istedigine bagli. Ornek vermek istedim.
The email describes concrete scenarios: vehicular at 100 km/h (3ms coherence) and stationary with moving surroundings (~200ms). The channel must be reconstructed before it decorrelates. This is compelling — but neither dataset captures it.

HRRR: static wind field, no user, no channel. RadioMapSeer: static pathloss map, no mobility, no time dimension. Neither dataset has a “fast-moving object trying to reconstruct something before a deadline.”

What I’d like to clarify: For these scenarios, which dataset should we use? Do we need a time-varying channel dataset (e.g., QuaDRiGa or DeepMIMO), or are we using HRRR/RadioMapSeer as proxies and artificially imposing latency constraints?
3

How can a router justify its overhead when models are already this fast?

From: Koyuncu, Erdem
RF projesi Apr 1
Mehmet Merhaba,

Sabah bahsettigimiz gibi RF problemi uzerinde calisir misin? Bilginer su sekilde modeller train etti: Modele 4 sample giriyor, butun field reconstruct ediliyor. Senin router modeliyle bu modellerde early exit yapacagiz…. Sorun olursa lutfen sor.

Aslinda bu konuda baska modeller de var onlari da egitebiliriz…

Bilginer, dataset vs yi Mehmet’e tanitir misin?
From: Dulkadiroglu, Mehmet
Re: RF projesi Apr 5
Hocam merhaba,

Routing setup’imizdaki motivasyonla ilgili bir soru sormak istiyorum.

Bilginer’in makalesinde Tablo 1’de bahsettigi inference sureleri halihazirda oldukca kisa gorunuyor. Mesela REPA 32’lik bir batchte yaklasik 59 milisaniye suruyor (sample basina ~1.9 ms). Diger bir yontem olan ET’de sample basina ~3 milisaniye suruyor.

Router ekligimiz durumda onun da ayri latency’si ve complexity’si olacak. Bu tradeoff’un hangi senaryoda anlamli bir avantaj saglayabilecegini netlestirmek istedim. Bu konuda aklinizda real-time, streaming veya repeated inference use case’i var mi?
From: Koyuncu, Erdem
Re: RF projesi Apr 6
Real-time diyelim… Mesela Antenler olctukce real time butun field’i reconstruct ediyoruz. Bahsettigin sureler aslinda uzun sureler kanalin degisim hizini dusundun zaman. <Channel coherence time< diye yazip kisaca okuyabilirsiniz. Kanal coherence’i kaybettikten sonra zaten field’i reconstruct etmenin bir anlami yok.
From: Koyuncu, Erdem
Re: RF projesi (follow-up) Apr 6
Pratik olarak kanallarin coherence time’i 1/doppler spread. Yani sen ne kadar hareketliysen kanal o kadar az coherent. Mesela 100 km/saat hizla bir arabanin icindeysen senin icin kanal 3 milisaniye coherent ancak, dolayisiyla kanali yeniden olcmen lazim.

Bir yerde oturuyorsan tabii ki doppler yok, ama etraftaki tipler hareket ettigi icin kanal yine de degisiyor, yani tas catlasin birkac yuz milisaniye coherence time alirsin. Yani cok cok buyuk bir marjin yok.

Inference time 2 saniyeyse mesela ve coherence time 200ms ise Sen inference yapana kadar kanal degisiyor… Real time bir sey yapmak istiyorsan neural networkun cop…. Tabii ne yapmak istedigine bagli. Ornek vermek istedim.
The professor’s example uses a 2-second inference time to motivate the problem — but the actual models run at 1–3 ms per sample, which is 1000× faster than that example. At these speeds, the router’s own overhead becomes the bottleneck.

Beyond latency: how does the router decide? If it routes based on coherence time (a known system parameter), no ML is needed — just a threshold. If it routes based on sample difficulty, the router only sees 4–16 sparse patches (2–6% of the field). Detecting difficulty from so little information is extremely challenging — even in NLP with full text input, task-difficulty routing remains an open problem.

What I’d like to clarify: What signal does the router use to make its decision? And given the ~2ms baseline, what is the target latency range where routing provides a meaningful improvement?
04

The Disconnect

The coherence time motivation assumes a time-varying channel with strict inference deadlines. Neither dataset has this property.

🚗

Vehicular

∼3 ms

At 100 km/h, the channel decorrelates in about 3 milliseconds. Inference must complete before then.

🚶

Pedestrian

∼50 ms

Walking speed gives more headroom, but the channel still evolves continuously.

💻

Stationary (indoor)

∼200 ms

No user movement, but surrounding objects move. Maximum coherence time — still finite.

HRRR Wind  STATIC

  • Hourly snapshots, no temporal sequence
  • No mobility or Doppler
  • No natural inference deadline
  • Wind changes on scales of minutes–hours

RadioMapSeer  STATIC

  • Fixed environment, computed once
  • No channel variation over time
  • No natural inference deadline
  • Ray-tracing output, not measurements
The core mismatch: Coherence time creates genuine latency pressure only when the channel is actively changing. Both datasets are static — you can take as long as you want to reconstruct them. Any latency budget we impose would be artificial, and reviewers will likely question why speed matters for static reconstruction.
Questions for discussion:
  • Should we use a time-varying channel simulator (QuaDRiGa, DeepMIMO, Sionna) to match the coherence time motivation?
  • Or are HRRR / RadioMapSeer intended as proxies for the spatial reconstruction task? If so, how do we frame the speed motivation for reviewers?
05

Router Feasibility

Even if we accept the latency motivation, the practical challenges of routing are significant given already-fast baseline models.

Model Inference / sample Parameters Fits in 3ms? (vehicular) Fits in 200ms? (stationary)
PINN ~0.8 ms 39M Yes Yes
REPA ~1.9 ms 35M Barely Yes
VMD ~1.9 ms 37M Barely Yes
ET ~3.0 ms 5M No Yes

1 Router overhead

A router network adds its own inference latency. Even 0.2ms overhead is a 10% tax on REPA's 1.9ms. The net time savings from routing to a faster model must exceed this overhead — and the margins are razor-thin at the millisecond scale.

2 Difficulty detection from sparse input

The router must decide which model to use based on 4–16 observed patches (2–6% of the field). Estimating reconstruction difficulty from so little information is an open problem. Even in NLP with rich text, task-difficulty routing is challenging.

3 Coherence time is already known

If routing is based on the coherence time (derived from Doppler spread), no learned router is needed — the system already knows the deadline. A simple threshold or lookup table suffices. This is practical but may not be publishable as a research contribution.

Questions for discussion:
  • Is the routing decision based on coherence time (a known system parameter) or estimated sample difficulty (must be learned)?
  • Would early-exit within a single model (stop at transformer layer 8 vs. 16) be more practical than routing between separate models? This avoids the overhead of a separate router.
  • What is the baseline we are trying to beat — always using the fast model (PINN) vs. always using the accurate model (REPA)?
06

Questions for Discussion

Concrete items to resolve before proceeding with implementation.

  1. Dataset choice

    Are HRRR and RadioMapSeer the final experimental datasets, or should we consider time-varying channel simulators (QuaDRiGa, DeepMIMO) to match the coherence time story?

  2. Routing trigger

    What decides which model to use — the coherence time (a known system parameter requiring no ML) or the estimated difficulty of the reconstruction input (requires a learned router)?

  3. Routing vs. early exit

    Given very fast baseline inference (1–3ms), would early-exit within one model avoid the overhead problem of a separate router? This changes the architecture and the research contribution.

  4. Target scenario

    What is the deployment scenario — vehicular, pedestrian, indoor? This determines the coherence time range and the reconstruction requirements. It also determines whether the ~2ms inference of current models is already fast enough.

  5. Target venue & timeline

    What conference or journal are we targeting, and what is the submission deadline? This shapes the scope of experiments we can run.

RF Project — Open Questions • Mehmet Dulkadiroglu • April 2026
